heading: Phase 1: Core Concepts (Training Plan)
content: Two caching methods: Automatic caching uses a single cache_control field at the top level of the request; the system auto-applies the breakpoint to the last cacheable block and moves it forward as the conversation grows, and is the best starting point. Explicit caching places cache_control on individual content blocks for fine-grained control, needed when parts of a prompt change at different frequencies, e.g. tools rarely change but context updates daily. Prefix hierarchy: cache prefixes build in strict order, tools then system then messages; static reusable content belongs at the beginning, with the cache breakpoint on the last block that stays identical across requests. The 20-block lookback rule: when a request arrives, the system hashes the prompt at the breakpoint and, if there is no direct match, walks backward one block at a time looking for a previous cache write, but stops after 20 blocks; if a growing conversation pushes the breakpoint 20-plus blocks past the last write, the system misses it and charges for a fresh expensive cache write. Pricing multipliers: cache reads cost only 10 percent of base input token price (the actual savings), 5-minute-TTL cache writes cost 25 percent more than base input price, and 1-hour-TTL cache writes cost 2x base input price.

heading: Phase 2: Testing Parameters and Audit Framework
content: Monitor the usage field on every API response: cache_creation_input_tokens (tokens written to cache, the upfront cost), cache_read_input_tokens (tokens successfully read from cache, proof caching is working), input_tokens (tokens after the last breakpoint, billed normally). Test by sending an identical prompt twice: the first response should show high cache_creation_input_tokens, the second should show 0 creation tokens and high cache_read_input_tokens. Cache invalidation triggers to deliberately test: changing a tool definition (name, description, or parameter) invalidates the entire cache (tools, system, and messages); adding or removing an image invalidates the message-blocks cache; modifying tool_choice also invalidates message blocks. Minimum token limits: the system silently ignores cache requests on prompts that are too short, e.g. Claude 3.5 Sonnet-class models need a minimum of 1,024 tokens to cache, while Opus/Haiku-class models need 4,096 tokens per this conversation (cross-check against the official pricing table). Test by sending a short prompt with a cache_control marker; both creation and read tokens will be 0 if it was too short. Cache Diagnostics (beta) lets the API compare consecutive requests and report exactly where the prompt prefix diverged, useful if cache_read_input_tokens keeps coming back 0 unexpectedly.

heading: Deep Dive: What Exactly Is a Block
content: A block is an individual element within the API request's arrays: a specific tool definition in tools, a specific system instruction in system, or a user message, assistant reply, image, or tool result within messages. Going past the 20-block window means the last cache write is now 21-plus blocks behind the current breakpoint; the system gives up looking, treats it as a cache miss, and reprocesses the entire prompt as a fresh expensive write. Detecting a miss: watch cache_read_input_tokens (0 when expecting a hit, with high cache_creation_input_tokens, means a miss occurred) or use Cache Diagnostics. Fixing it has two options: Manual/Explicit caching allows up to 4 cache breakpoints per request, so add a second breakpoint closer to the end of the new messages before the first one falls out of the 20-block window; Automatic caching (recommended for most cases) needs just one marker at the top of the request, with the system moving the breakpoint to the last eligible block on every new message, requiring no manual block-counting.

heading: Minimum Token Threshold Clarified
content: The 1,024-token (or 4,096 for some models) minimum applies to the entire cumulative prompt up to the breakpoint, meaning system instructions plus tool definitions plus all previous messages summed together, not each individual new message. Once the cumulative total crosses the threshold, everything gets cached and small subsequent messages just get appended, with the automatic breakpoint continuing to move forward. There is no error or warning if a prompt is too short; check cache_creation_input_tokens and cache_read_input_tokens, both 0 meaning not cached. Pro tip from the source doc: if a prompt falls just short of the minimum (e.g. 900 tokens), it can be worth padding it (more system instructions or examples) to cross the threshold, since the resulting 90 percent discount on cache reads outweighs the padding cost for frequently-reused prompts.

heading: Cache Portability: Cannot Export, Save, or Migrate
content: The cache is strictly in-memory only; the system computes Key-Value (KV) representations and cryptographic hashes held in active memory, never stored at rest (no hard drive, no database). It cannot be exported, downloaded, or handed off, and is strictly isolated per workspace and tied to the specific model that computed it -- a cache built cheaply on one model cannot be transferred to a different, smarter model. There is no Anthropic feature (the user asked about an ANT feature / Managed Agents) that bypasses this. When starting a new session or day, there is no memory of the previous cache; the correct approach is to carry over the source text (the compiled file), not the cache itself: re-upload the file, re-enable caching, pay once for a fresh cache write, then get discounted reads for the rest of that session.

heading: Compaction: The Right Use for an Agent
content: A growing project cannot be manually forced to break every 20 blocks (the hard cap is 4 explicit breakpoints per request, and automatic caching already handles this dynamically). Instead, the recommended pattern, which the source documentation calls Compaction, is to have an agent periodically summarize or rewrite older parts of a conversation into a concise document, then place that streamlined static content at the very beginning of the prompt array for the system to cache. This keeps token count down and caching efficient as a project scales.

heading: Multi-Facet Projects: Modular Caching With the 4 Breakpoints
content: For a large multifaceted project, use the 4 explicit breakpoints to maintain separate pillars, e.g. Module 1 for core project instructions/SOPs, Module 2 for a dataset/codebase summary for Facet A, Module 3 for a summary for Facet B. Each module gets its own breakpoint. Benefit: if Facet A's code updates, only that block's hash changes and gets invalidated, while the other modules (core instructions, Facet B) keep their 90-percent-discounted cached reads intact. The 1-hour TTL is recommended specifically when an agentic side-agent will take longer than 5 minutes to complete a processing loop, so large project documents stay warm in memory between interactions. Always place the heaviest, most static context at the very top of the prompt since prefixes build top-down.

heading: Sub-Agents and Shared Caching
content: Sub-agents can use prompt caching; it is not restricted to the main orchestrating CLI. Caches are tied to the workspace, not to an individual script or agent instance, so any agent in that workspace can read a shared cache as long as their prompt prefixes are 100 percent identical up to the breakpoint. The concurrency trap: a cache entry only becomes readable after the first API response begins; if a main CLI spins up 4 sub-agents simultaneously and they all fire requests at the same millisecond, none finds an existing cache, and all 4 process from scratch, getting charged for 4 separate expensive cache writes instead of 1. The fix is Cache Pre-warming: (1) Pre-warm -- the main CLI sends a request containing all shared project data/sub-agent instructions with max_tokens set to 0, reading the prompt into the model and writing the cache at the breakpoint while returning immediately with no generated output text; (2) Wait for that pre-warm request to complete so the cache is established; (3) Execute -- spin up the sub-agents in parallel, all hitting the now-warm, heavily-discounted cache read simultaneously. Bundling directions for multiple sub-agents: since a cache hit requires a 100 percent identical prefix up to the breakpoint, the practical pattern is one giant system prompt containing the shared project context and baseline instructions for all sub-agents, with the breakpoint at the end of that block; each sub-agent then appends only its unique task as the final user message, letting all sub-agents share one cached prefix even though their individual tasks differ. Confirmed discount math: cache reads are 10 percent (0.1x) of base input price, a 90 percent discount; 5-minute cache writes cost 1.25x base price; 1-hour cache writes cost 2x base price. Every cache-read hit resets/refreshes the 5-minute TTL for free, so as long as an agent calls at least every roughly 4.9 minutes, the cache stays alive indefinitely without another write cost.

heading: Multi-Day Projects and Mixed TTLs
content: Caches do not survive across multiple days; a 1-hour cache expires 1 hour after last use, and if a project pauses overnight, all cached segments are gone by the next day. On a later day, the system has completely forgotten the cache: the first request that day is a fresh expensive write, and subsequent requests during that day's session are discounted reads, as long as no more than an hour passes between calls. Mixing TTLs in one prompt is allowed but with a strict rule: cache entries with a longer TTL must appear before shorter TTLs in the prompt structure, so 1-hour segments (e.g. core project guidelines that update slowly) go at the top, with 5-minute segments (e.g. a specific sub-agent's fast-changing task) placed below them. The confirmed daily workflow: (1) end of day, an agent reviews the day's thread, condenses progress, updates code/data pillars, and exports everything to concise local text files; (2) start of next day, those updated compiled files are injected at the top of a new prompt and sent via Cache Pre-warming (max_tokens 0) to rebuild the cache before sub-agents start; (3) daily operations, sub-agents fire in parallel against the now-warm cache all day, getting reduced latency and the 90 percent token discount.

heading: Terminology Clarification and Other Platform Features
content: Schema in AI/programming usually means strict data structures (tool definitions, JSON output formats), not what the user meant by organizing rules and setup. The correct terms: System Prompt / System Instructions is the organized set of rules/setup/baseline directions for agents, placed at the top of the request; Static Prefix / Cache Prefix is that same block once a cache breakpoint is placed at its end, static because it stays identical across requests, letting the system read it from memory instantly. Beyond prompt caching, the Claude Console platform (per its navigation) includes Advanced Tool Use (Web search tool, Code execution tool, Memory tool, Bash tool, Text editor tool, Computer use tool), Workspaces (strict organizational/security boundary, caches isolated per workspace), Extended Thinking and Control (Extended thinking, Adaptive thinking, Task budgets beta, Fast mode research preview), Context Management (Compaction, Context editing, Mid-conversation system messages, Cache diagnostics beta), Message Batch Processing (Batch API discounts stack with prompt-caching multipliers for bulk async work), Files (Files API, native PDF support, image/vision capabilities), and Model Context Protocol (Remote MCP servers, MCP connectors, MCP tunnels for hooking Claude to local file systems/databases).

heading: Direct Relevance: The User's Own MCP Plus On-Device Assistant Question
content: The user directly asked in this conversation whether they could build an on-device mobile assistant with API hooks, cloud hooks, and agentic device controls, and specifically whether they could create an MCP connection between their CLI and their on-device Kotlin app -- this is the exact Omni Claw architecture question. NotebookLM's answer: theoretically yes, MCP's standard client-server architecture would let a CLI act as the client requesting context/triggering actions while the Kotlin app acts as an MCP server exposing local mobile API hooks/device controls, but the loaded source documents (focused on prompt caching) did not contain the actual MCP technical/architecture docs or Kotlin specifics, so this was flagged as needing independent verification/further research rather than answered definitively.

heading: The Payoff: Building an LLM Wiki as the Cacheable System Prompt
content: This is the most directly relevant thread in the conversation: NotebookLM's own recommendation converges exactly on the wiki system already being built. The Agent CLI should load a centralized Wiki (core caching rules, SOPs, shared project context) at the absolute beginning of every prompt, since caching builds prefixes top-down (tools, system, messages) and the thickest/most stable content needs to go first for all sub-agents to share the same cached sequence. Every day, the Agent CLI takes this Wiki (plus updated code/data) and sends it via Cache Pre-warming (max_tokens 0) to write it into the 1-hour extended cache once; from then on the main CLI and all sub-agents read it at a 90 percent discount. The Wiki also functions as a rulebook preventing accidental cache invalidation (reminding the CLI not to casually change tool definitions or add/remove images mid-session) and as a breakpoint-management guide (if using explicit caching, the Wiki's own content occupies the first of 4 breakpoint slots, leaving 3 for modular data pillars). NotebookLM explicitly generated a Technical Wiki Tailored Report for the user's Agent CLI during this session, described as ready to export and plug directly into the system as its core rulebook -- a NotebookLM-native artifact, separate from and prior to the actual OpenWiki/OB1/reasoning-bank system being built in the current Claude Code session, but independently validating the same design: the wiki IS the cache prefix.

heading: Relevance to This Project
content: This document is a direct, load-bearing justification for the OpenWiki plus OB1 plus reasoning-bank memory system already being built: it confirms, from Anthropic's own documented caching mechanics rather than just architectural intuition, that a well-maintained, static, top-loaded wiki document is precisely the artifact that makes prompt caching cost-effective for a multi-agent CLI. Concretely: the eventual Omni Claw / Agent CLI system prompt should load the wiki/skill content as the first, most-static block of any request; a daily compile-and-pre-warm cycle (agent condenses progress, exports to text, next session pre-warms the cache with max_tokens 0) is the correct operational pattern for keeping a long-running project's context both cheap and fast; if/when sub-agents are spun up (matching the dual-agent and 3-device neural mesh architectures documented elsewhere in this project), they should share one pre-warmed cache rather than firing concurrent cold requests; and the MCP question the user asked here (CLI-as-client, Kotlin-app-as-MCP-server) is still open and worth resolving directly against MCP's official spec rather than this NotebookLM session's admitted guess.
